literal: the all-pervader; from √viṣ 'to pervade, to enter into'
invariant_definition: The deity who, in the Vaiṣṇava Vedāntic schools (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita, Acintya-Bhedābheda, Śuddhādvaita, Dvaitādvaita), is identified with *brahman* in its highest description. Originally one of the Vedic Ādityas, expanded in the *Itihāsas* and *Purāṇas* to the supreme cosmic deity manifesting through *avatāras* (Daśāvatāra, including Rāma and Kṛṣṇa). All Vedāntic schools accept Viṣṇu as a real divine power; the Vaiṣṇava schools elevate Viṣṇu to ultimate-reality status; Advaita and Śaiva-influenced schools treat Viṣṇu as one *iṣṭa-devatā* among others, all reducible to (or expressing) *brahman*.

per_school.definition: Viṣṇu is one *saguṇa* form of *brahman*; an excellent *iṣṭa-devatā* and object of *upāsana*, but not above other forms in the highest analysis (*nirguṇa brahman* exceeds all forms). Śaṅkara is reported to have practiced *pañcāyatana-pūjā* worshipping five forms (Śiva, Viṣṇu, Devī, Sūrya, Gaṇeśa) as equally valid.
Viṣṇu (= Nārāyaṇa) IS *brahman* in his highest description — possessor of infinite auspicious qualities, with *cit* and *acit* as his body. The exclusive identification is constitutive.
Viṣṇu is the unique *svatantra* — the only independent reality. Madhva develops a Viṣṇu-supremacy thesis (*Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya*) against all rival identifications.

Viṣṇu (in his various forms — Nārāyaṇa, Vāsudeva, etc.) is *paramātman*, an aspect of Kṛṣṇa who is *svayaṃ-bhagavān* (Bhāgavata 1.3.28). Kṛṣṇa is the *avatārī*; Viṣṇu and his *avatāras* are *avatāras*.

translator_note: Untranslatable as a proper noun. Resist 'the Hindu god of preservation' (the schoolbook gloss) — for the Vaiṣṇava Vedāntins Viṣṇu is the unique highest reality, not one of three gods in a division-of-labor. The Trimūrti scheme (Brahmā-creator, Viṣṇu-preserver, Śiva-destroyer) is a Purāṇic mythological convention; it does NOT govern the Vaiṣṇava-Vedāntin metaphysics.
